author: @spelunkerd
text: Why do you need to cut the drum of the rotor into a bar shape? Wouldn't the rotation of the drum through the magnetic field be enough to start up a counter emf inside the drum, then induce electron movement in the adjacent coil?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's to do with flux path - if it is still a  drum there is no high and low point in the flux path

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text: With boost converter you can up the voltage to charge just about anything. There is some losses in conversion but nothing's perfect.

author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
Those fan blades make great Peloton Wheels for using water based generation. I am working on a portable hydroelectric system that uses a hydraulic ram pump in a basin with a barrel reservoir to create a closed hydro system and having the gravity from the water flowing downward to the work to generate electricity.

I'm nearing completion and would love to show you when it is finished. 

Well done mate, love the magnetic flux series. Can you use the flux to make retractable items, or have you given that a think?

replies.author: @sailingoctopus1
replies.text: Sorry to be pedantic, but a Pelton Wheel is quite different. It's actually more like a Francis turbine, but really you might more properly describe it more generically as an impulse wheel. In practice, you'll find it is nowhere near as efficient as a Pelton Wheel or a Francis Turbine, mainly because the water does not get out of the way efficiently, after having delivered its kinetic energy to the wheel.

replies.author: @ryanlebeck259
replies.text:
@sailingoctopus1 I appreciate the feedback, but not matter what the water generates it will still charge a battery for general uses.

It's not the efficiency that I'm looking for, it is proof of concept. Improvements can come after 😁

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: I think for this purpose you can cut the shaded pole winding out. I't will prevent loses there and it will perform a bit better.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
I commended somthing similar. 

As a motor, the shaded pole coil creates a rotating magnetic field in one direction through a phase delay in the magnetic field on one side of each pole.   Now I wonder what it will do  when operated as a reluctance generator. 

i suspect the wave form will differ rotating clockwise from counterclockwise. I need to get a cheap oscilloscope now. 😁

author: @AdricM

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
Super cool.  

Tangentally, I'm still curious if a generator with no moving parts could be created by, instead of a rotor, a permanent magnet assist electromagnet was used (or several in sequence) to cause the change in the magnetic field, just getting the flux lines to redirect into and out of the generator coils.  like, turning the permanent magnet's "On and off" (redirecting their flux) inside a generator-  Doing the rotor thing, Strictly with moving the magnetic flux of permanent magnets.    In this, I Think, may be a chance for "Overunity".  Not really, but a way to hack physics sideways to make the magnets do the thing without having to actually move them.  Wobbling magnetic flux, seems like it would be less work and faster than physically moving the magnet, or redirecting the flux by spinning a rotor.  It seems like you could, potentially, put less energy into wobbling the electromagnetic flux, than the permanent magnets induced in the coils.  Does anyone know, how much electricity does it take to electromagnetically wobble a permanent magnet's flux vs spinning a rotor?  I've not figured any way to ask google this question that returned Anything useful.  
I am cognizant of my ignorance in how to actually accomplish that.    Your last several videos you've touched on these concepts, but from a different fundamental use perspective.  I dunno.  Thank you for making me think, as always.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: sounds like you can try this with a transformer. Add perminate magnets and alternate current in the primary and see what happens on the secondary. I'd wager though that you wont get over unity but there might be something interesting in the wave form due to magnetic saturation in one direction.

replies.author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
replies.text: Not sure that aligns with my though here.  or maybe I'm not thinking on it right.  What would a magnetic assist on the primary of a transformer DO?   Doesn't that just increase or decrease the voltage, per turns of coil?  ... I don't think (and I may be wrong) that would test what i'm pointing at.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@ryanjamesloyd6733 the voltage on the secondary is a function of the rate of change in flux and the number of turns.  Seeing as  perminate
 magnetic's magnetic  field  may nearly saturate the core, there may be little inductive reactance to current in one direction on the primary and lots in another the other direction. I'm just saying. that might be interesting.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@ryanjamesloyd6733 as to not sure it aligns with your thoughts.....  let me try this.  A pair magnets outside with one on each side of a transformer  may  conduct  some of their magnetic circuit though the center of a primary and secondary  coils. then when adding current to the primary, depending on it direction, it may either assist or eject the perminate magnetic's field lines from the transformer's secondary windings. 

I dont expect over Unity but I think there's something interesting here.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @ryanjamesloyd6733  somewhat related and also interesting  is using a DC bias in saturatabe reactor.
